---
title: "Chapter 43 — 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 42 · 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps](ch42.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [44 · 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability →](ch44.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Modern AI models are only as useful as the hardware and systems that run them. This chapter is about the engineering underneath the model: the chips that do the math, why a model that "fits" on paper still won't run, how to spread a giant model across many machines for training, and the bag of tricks that make inference fast and cheap enough to actually serve. It sits at the production end of the field — once you have a trained model, this is what turns it into something a user can query.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Production & systems engineering for AI · used to train and serve models efficiently · the one key idea: **memory and data movement, not raw FLOPs, are usually the real bottleneck**.
:::

## 43.1 — Why parallel hardware: GPUs, TPUs, and CPUs

Start with the shape of the work. Training and running a neural network is, at bottom, a flood of matrix multiplications and elementwise operations applied to huge arrays of numbers. The same operation is repeated across millions of elements with no dependency between them. That is the textbook case for **data parallelism in hardware**: do many identical operations at once.

A **CPU** (central processing unit) is built for the opposite job — a few very fast cores with deep caches and clever branch prediction, optimized for complicated, sequential control flow. It can do matrix math, but with only a handful of cores it processes the array a sliver at a time. A **GPU** (graphics processing unit) flips the tradeoff: thousands of simple arithmetic cores running the same instruction on different data (the **SIMT** model — single instruction, multiple threads). For a big matrix multiply, the GPU's thousands of lanes crush the CPU's dozens.

A **TPU** (tensor processing unit, Google's custom chip) goes further in one direction. Its heart is a **systolic array**: a hard-wired grid of multiply-accumulate units where data flows through and partial sums accumulate in place, so a matrix multiply happens without constantly shuttling intermediate values back to memory. That makes it extremely efficient for the dense linear algebra of deep learning, at the cost of flexibility. GPUs and TPUs are both **accelerators**; the CPU is usually still present as the **host**, orchestrating work and feeding data.

Picture the systolic array as a grid through which two streams flow and meet:

<svg viewBox="0 0 320 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="9">
  <text x="8" y="14">weights →</text>
  <text x="8" y="150">activations ↑</text>
  <g fill="none" stroke="#888" stroke-width="1">
    <rect x="120" y="30" width="28" height="28"/><rect x="152" y="30" width="28" height="28"/><rect x="184" y="30" width="28" height="28"/>
    <rect x="120" y="62" width="28" height="28"/><rect x="152" y="62" width="28" height="28"/><rect x="184" y="62" width="28" height="28"/>
    <rect x="120" y="94" width="28" height="28"/><rect x="152" y="94" width="28" height="28"/><rect x="184" y="94" width="28" height="28"/>
  </g>
  <text x="128" y="48" fill="#4a90d9">MAC</text><text x="160" y="48" fill="#4a90d9">MAC</text><text x="192" y="48" fill="#4a90d9">MAC</text>
  <text x="128" y="80" fill="#4a90d9">MAC</text><text x="160" y="80" fill="#4a90d9">MAC</text><text x="192" y="80" fill="#4a90d9">MAC</text>
  <text x="128" y="112" fill="#4a90d9">MAC</text><text x="160" y="112" fill="#4a90d9">MAC</text><text x="192" y="112" fill="#4a90d9">MAC</text>
  <text x="232" y="80" fill="#2a8">partial sums accumulate in place</text>
</svg>

| Processor | Cores / style | Best at | Weakness |
|-----------|---------------|---------|----------|
| CPU | Few, complex, sequential | Control flow, latency-sensitive small jobs | Massive parallel math |
| GPU | Thousands of SIMT lanes | Dense matrix/tensor ops, flexible | Power, cost, programming model |
| TPU | Systolic MAC array | Huge dense matmuls at scale | Less flexible, ecosystem-locked |

::: {.callout-tip}
Rule of thumb: if the computation is "the same arithmetic on a giant array, with no branching," it belongs on an accelerator. If it's "messy logic with lots of `if`s," the CPU wins. Most ML pipelines need both.
:::

## 43.2 — Memory is the real bottleneck

Newcomers assume the limiting factor is raw compute — **FLOPs**, floating-point operations per second. In practice the wall you hit first is usually **memory**: how much fits, and how fast you can move it.

There are two distinct memory problems. The first is **capacity**. Model weights, the activations you must keep for the backward pass, the optimizer state, and (at inference) the KV cache all live in the accelerator's high-speed memory (**HBM**, high-bandwidth memory). A 70-billion-parameter model in 16-bit precision needs $70 \times 10^9 \times 2 \approx 140$ GB just for weights — more than a single 80 GB GPU holds. It simply does not fit.

The second problem is **bandwidth**. Many operations are **memory-bound**, not compute-bound: the arithmetic units sit idle waiting for data to arrive from HBM. The diagnostic is **arithmetic intensity** — FLOPs performed per byte read from memory. If you do little math per byte (as in much of autoregressive decoding, where you stream a huge weight matrix to multiply against a single token's vector), you are bandwidth-limited and the chip's peak FLOPs are irrelevant.

$$\text{Arithmetic intensity} = \frac{\text{FLOPs}}{\text{bytes moved}}$$

A useful mental model is the **roofline**: plot achievable performance against arithmetic intensity and you get two regimes. Below a turning point, performance rises with intensity — you are under the slanted "bandwidth roof," starved for data. Above it, you flatten out against the horizontal "compute roof" set by peak FLOPs. Operations like a single token's matrix-vector product live far to the left (low intensity, bandwidth-bound); a large batched matrix multiply lives to the right (compute-bound). Optimization is largely the art of pushing a kernel rightward — doing more math per byte you fetch.

This is why the famous **memory hierarchy** matters. On-chip SRAM (registers, shared memory) is tiny but blisteringly fast; HBM is large but ~10× slower; host RAM and disk are larger still and far slower. A huge amount of systems work — fused kernels like **FlashAttention**, careful tiling, keeping data resident — is really about avoiding trips down this hierarchy.

```python
# Two views of the same 70B model — which limit hits first?
params = 70e9
bytes_per_param = 2          # fp16/bf16
weight_gb = params * bytes_per_param / 1e9   # ~140 GB -> capacity wall
gpu_hbm_gb = 80
print(weight_gb > gpu_hbm_gb)  # True: needs >1 GPU before any speed talk
```

::: {.callout-warning}
Comparing two chips by their headline TFLOPS is a classic mistake. If your workload is memory-bound — and LLM decoding usually is — the chip with more **memory bandwidth** wins, even if it has fewer FLOPs.
:::

## 43.3 — Distributed training: splitting the model and the data

When a model or its training batch outgrows one device, you spread the work across many. There are four canonical ways to cut the problem, and large runs combine all of them.

- **Data parallelism**: every device holds a full copy of the model and processes a different slice of the batch. After each step they exchange gradients (an **all-reduce**) and average them. Simple and scalable — until the model itself no longer fits on one device.
- **Model (tensor) parallelism**: a single layer's math is split across devices. A big matrix multiply is partitioned column- or row-wise, each device computes its shard, and partial results are combined. This shrinks per-device memory but demands fast interconnects because devices must communicate *inside* every layer.
- **Pipeline parallelism**: different *layers* live on different devices, like stations on an assembly line. Layer group 1 on GPU A hands activations to group 2 on GPU B. The danger is the **pipeline bubble** — idle devices waiting for the first microbatch to arrive — which is mitigated by splitting the batch into **microbatches** so the pipeline stays full.
- **Expert (MoE) parallelism**: in a mixture-of-experts model, different expert sub-networks live on different devices, and a router sends each token only to the experts it needs. This scales total parameters without scaling per-token compute, but adds an all-to-all communication step to shuffle tokens to their experts and back.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  subgraph Data["Data parallel"]
    d1[full model · batch slice A] --- d2[full model · batch slice B]
  end
  subgraph Tensor["Tensor parallel"]
    t1[layer shard 1] --- t2[layer shard 2]
  end
  subgraph Pipe["Pipeline parallel"]
    p1[layers 1-8] --> p2[layers 9-16]
  end
```

The breakthrough that made trillion-token training routine is **ZeRO** (Zero Redundancy Optimizer) and its PyTorch-native cousin **FSDP** (Fully Sharded Data Parallel). Plain data parallelism is wasteful: every device stores a redundant full copy of the weights, gradients, and optimizer state. ZeRO/FSDP **shard** these across devices — each holds only $1/N$ of the parameters and optimizer state — and gathers the full set for a layer just-in-time during the forward and backward pass, then frees it. You get data-parallel simplicity with a fraction of the per-device memory, paying in extra communication.

A complementary memory-saver is **activation checkpointing** (also called gradient checkpointing): instead of storing every layer's activations for the backward pass, you keep only a few and recompute the rest on the fly. It trades extra compute for a large drop in memory — often the difference between a batch fitting or not.

The cost that ties all of this together is the **interconnect**: high-speed links like NVLink within a node and InfiniBand across nodes. Tensor parallelism is communication-heavy and wants the fastest links (kept inside one node); pipeline and data parallelism tolerate slower cross-node links. Choosing the parallelism layout *is* choosing how to map communication onto your network topology.

## 43.4 — Mixed precision and number formats

The numbers in a network don't all need full precision. **Mixed-precision training** does most of the math in 16-bit floats while keeping a few critical pieces in 32-bit, cutting memory and roughly doubling throughput on hardware with dedicated low-precision units (tensor cores).

The subtlety is *which* 16-bit format. A floating-point number splits its bits between an **exponent** (which sets the range — how large or small a value it can represent) and a **mantissa** (which sets the precision — how many significant digits). **FP16** spends its 16 bits on a narrow exponent and a wider mantissa, so small gradients underflow to zero and training diverges. **BF16** (bfloat16) keeps FP32's full exponent range but only 7 mantissa bits — it trades precision for range, which is exactly the right trade for deep learning, where range matters more than the last few digits. BF16 is now the default on modern accelerators.

| Format | Bits | Range | Precision | Typical use |
|--------|------|-------|-----------|-------------|
| FP32 | 32 | Wide | High | Master weights, reductions |
| FP16 | 16 | Narrow | Medium | Older mixed precision (needs loss scaling) |
| BF16 | 16 | Wide | Lower | Modern training default |
| FP8 | 8 | Narrow | Very low | Frontier training/inference on newest hardware |

When FP16 is used, **loss scaling** rescues the small gradients: multiply the loss by a large factor before backprop so gradients land in FP16's representable range, then divide it back out before the weight update. The optimizer keeps a **master copy of weights in FP32** so tiny updates aren't lost to rounding. BF16's wider range mostly removes the need for loss scaling, which is part of why it won. The newest accelerators push further to **FP8** for the heaviest matrix multiplies, recovering accuracy with per-tensor scaling factors — the same range-vs-precision tension, one step more aggressive.

::: {.callout-tip}
"Mixed" is the operative word: compute in 16-bit for speed, but accumulate and store the master weights in 32-bit so small updates don't vanish into rounding error.
:::

## 43.5 — Shrinking the model: quantization, pruning, distillation

Training is a one-time cost; inference is paid on every request, often billions of times. The dominant lever for cheaper inference is to make the model *smaller* without making it noticeably worse. Three families do this.

**Quantization** stores and computes weights (and sometimes activations) in fewer bits. The idea is a mapping from a floating-point range to a small set of integers: pick a **scale** $s$ so that $q = \text{round}(x / s)$ and recover $\hat{x} = q \cdot s$. Going from 16-bit to **int8** halves memory and speeds up memory-bound decoding; aggressive **4-bit** formats (like **NF4**, the normal-float-4 used in QLoRA) cut it further. **Post-training quantization** (PTQ) applies this to an already-trained model with little or no retraining; schemes such as **GPTQ** (which minimizes the output error layer by layer) and **AWQ** (which protects the small set of "salient" weight channels that matter most) push to 4 bits with little quality loss. When even that loses too much accuracy, **quantization-aware training** (QAT) simulates the rounding during training so the model learns weights that survive being quantized — more expensive, but it recovers quality at the lowest bit-widths.

```python
import numpy as np
# Symmetric int8 quantization of one weight tensor
w = np.array([-0.9, 0.1, 0.7, 0.33], dtype=np.float32)
s = np.abs(w).max() / 127           # scale maps max value to int8 range
q = np.round(w / s).astype(np.int8) # store these small ints, not floats
w_hat = q * s                       # dequantize when computing
print(q, np.abs(w - w_hat).max())   # tiny reconstruction error
```

**Pruning** removes weights outright. **Unstructured pruning** zeros individual low-magnitude weights, yielding a sparse matrix that needs special hardware support to actually run faster. **Structured pruning** removes whole units — neurons, attention heads, channels — producing a genuinely smaller dense model that any hardware speeds up. The bet is that large networks are over-parameterized and a sizable fraction of weights contribute little.

**Knowledge distillation** trains a small **student** model to imitate a large **teacher**. Instead of learning only from hard labels, the student matches the teacher's full output distribution (its **soft targets**, or "dark knowledge"), which carries richer signal — *how confident* the teacher was across classes. The result is a compact model that punches above its parameter count. These three techniques compose: a model can be distilled, pruned, and quantized in sequence.

::: {.callout-warning}
Quantization quality is uneven across a model. A few **outlier** weights and activations carry disproportionate signal; quantize them as crudely as everything else and accuracy collapses. That single observation is why methods like AWQ and per-channel scaling exist — naive uniform 4-bit rarely works on its own.
:::

## 43.6 — Serving LLMs fast: KV cache, batching, speculative decoding

Autoregressive generation has a punishing structure: to produce token $t$ you must have produced tokens $1 \ldots t-1$, one at a time. Naively, each new token would re-run attention over the entire prefix from scratch — quadratic, wasteful work.

The **KV cache** fixes the obvious redundancy. In attention, every past token contributes a **key** and **value** vector that never change once computed. So you compute them once and cache them; generating a new token only computes its own query and attends against the stored keys and values. This turns per-step work from "reprocess the whole sequence" into "process one token." The cost moves to memory: the cache grows with sequence length and batch size, and for long contexts it can dwarf the weights themselves. KV-cache size, not compute, is often what limits how many requests you can serve at once.

That memory pressure motivated **PagedAttention** (the technique behind **vLLM**). Classic serving pre-allocates one contiguous block per request sized for the maximum possible length, wasting most of it. PagedAttention borrows the operating system's idea of **virtual memory**: store the KV cache in small fixed-size **pages** allocated on demand, so memory is used only as the sequence actually grows. The reclaimed memory lets you fit far more concurrent requests.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TB
  R[New request arrives] --> Q{Free GPU slot?}
  Q -- yes --> A[Add to running batch mid-flight]
  Q -- no --> W[Wait in queue]
  A --> S[Step all sequences together one token]
  S --> D{Sequence finished?}
  D -- yes --> E[Evict · free its KV pages]
  D -- no --> S
  E --> Q
```

On top of this sits **continuous batching**. Traditional **static batching** waits to assemble a fixed group, runs them in lockstep, and can't start a new request until the whole batch finishes — so a long generation stalls everyone. Continuous batching instead adds and removes sequences from the running batch token by token: the moment one request finishes, its slot is freed and a waiting request joins immediately. Utilization stays high without making short requests wait behind long ones.

It helps to separate the two phases of serving. **Prefill** processes the entire prompt at once — a big, compute-bound matrix multiply that fills the KV cache. **Decode** then emits one token per step — small, memory-bound, and repeated hundreds of times. They have opposite hardware profiles, which is why serving systems increasingly schedule them separately (and why time-to-first-token, set by prefill, is a different number from time-per-output-token, set by decode).

Finally, **speculative decoding** attacks the one-token-at-a-time latency directly. A small, fast **draft** model proposes several tokens ahead; the large **target** model then verifies all of them in a single parallel forward pass. Because verifying $k$ tokens at once costs about the same as generating one (decoding is memory-bound, so the extra compute is nearly free), every accepted draft token is a token the big model didn't have to generate sequentially. Crucially, a rejection-sampling check guarantees the output distribution is **identical** to the target model's — it is a pure speedup, not an approximation.

## 43.7 — The economics: throughput, latency, cost, and where to serve

All these techniques exist to move two numbers, and they pull against each other. **Latency** is how long one user waits — often split into **time-to-first-token** (how fast the response starts, set mostly by prefill) and **time-per-output-token** (how fast it streams, set by decode). **Throughput** is total tokens per second across all users, which sets your cost per token. Bigger batches raise throughput (better hardware utilization) but raise each user's latency too. The right operating point depends on the product: a chatbot prioritizes low latency; a nightly batch-summarization job prioritizes throughput.

The cost math is concrete. A served deployment's cost per token is roughly the hourly price of the hardware divided by the tokens it produces per hour:

$$\text{cost per 1K tokens} = \frac{\text{GPU \$/hour}}{\text{tokens/hour}} \times 1000$$

A \$2/hour GPU pushing 3,600,000 tokens/hour costs about \$0.00056 per 1K tokens. Double the throughput with continuous batching and quantization and you've halved your unit cost — which is why all of §43.5 and §43.6 are, ultimately, cost-reduction engineering.

```python
gpu_cost_per_hour = 2.0
tokens_per_hour = 3_600_000        # ~1000 tok/s sustained
cost_per_1k = gpu_cost_per_hour / tokens_per_hour * 1000
print(round(cost_per_1k, 5))       # ~0.00056  -> batching/quant cut this
```

The last big choice is **where** the model runs. **Cloud serving** centralizes models on powerful accelerators: easy to scale and update, but every request pays network round-trip latency and ongoing GPU rental, and user data leaves the device. **Edge serving** runs a (usually smaller, quantized) model on the user's phone, laptop, or an on-prem box: near-zero network latency, strong privacy, and no per-token cloud bill, but constrained by the device's memory and power. The frontier is **hybrid** — a small edge model handles common cases instantly and escalates hard queries to a large cloud model — which is exactly why model-shrinking techniques matter beyond saving money: they're what make edge serving possible at all.

::: {.callout-tip}
When someone quotes "the cost of an LLM," ask which number they optimized. A throughput-tuned, quantized, continuously-batched deployment can be an order of magnitude cheaper per token than a naive one running the identical model.
:::

## 43.8 — Key takeaways

- Accelerators (GPU/TPU) win because deep learning is mostly the *same* arithmetic over huge arrays; CPUs win on branchy, sequential logic.
- The first wall is usually **memory** — capacity (does it fit in HBM?) and bandwidth (is the chip starved waiting for data?), not peak FLOPs. The **roofline** model tells you which regime you're in; LLM decoding is typically memory-bound.
- Scaling training means combining **data, tensor, pipeline, and expert parallelism**; **ZeRO/FSDP** shard weights, gradients, and optimizer state to remove redundant copies, and **activation checkpointing** trades compute for memory.
- **Mixed precision** (especially **BF16**, now **FP8** at the frontier) roughly doubles throughput and halves memory; keep an FP32 master weight copy so small updates survive.
- Cheaper inference comes from shrinking the model — **quantization** (int8, NF4, GPTQ, AWQ; PTQ vs QAT), **pruning**, **distillation** — which compose.
- Fast LLM serving rests on the **KV cache**, **PagedAttention/vLLM**, **continuous batching**, the **prefill vs decode** split, and **speculative decoding** (a guaranteed-equivalent speedup).
- Every technique trades on the **latency vs throughput** axis; the choice, and cloud-vs-edge, is ultimately cost and product engineering.

## 43.9 — See also

- **Chapter 18 — Attention & Transformers**: where the KV cache and FlashAttention come from.
- **Chapter 30 — Large Language Models**: the models this infrastructure exists to train and serve.
- **Chapter 31 — Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment**: QLoRA and NF4 quantization in the fine-tuning context.
- **Chapter 42 — MLOps & LLMOps**: the deployment, monitoring, and lifecycle layer above raw serving.
- **Chapter 15 — Training Deep Neural Networks**: mixed precision and optimization from the training-dynamics side.
- **Chapter 47 — Frontier & Emerging Directions**: new hardware and efficiency research beyond today's stack.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 42 · 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps](ch42.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [44 · 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability →](ch44.html)
